---
title: "BKP: An R Package for Beta Kernel Process Modeling"
shorttitle: "BKP"
# date: "2026.04.11"
header-includes:
  - \AtBeginDocument{\author[Jiangyan Zhao (ECNU)]{Jiangyan Zhao}}
  - \AtBeginDocument{\date[2026.07.24 (Kunming)]{2026.07.24}}
  - \renewcommand*{\bibfont}{\footnotesize}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \graphicspath{{../code/figure/}}
institute: 
  - School of Statistics, ECNU
  - jyzhao@sfs.ecnu.edu.cn
  - joint work with Kunhai Qing and Jin Xu
latex_engine: pdflatex
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    slide_level: 3
    keep_tex: true
    keep_md: true
    citation_package: natbib
    includes:
      in_header: preamble.tex
bibliography: ../paper/refs.bib
biblio-style: "chicago"
link-citations: true
classoption: "aspectratio=169"
# colorlinks: true    # 启用链接颜色
# citecolor: blue     # 设置引文链接颜色（如 blue, red, green, magenta）
# urlcolor: blue      # 设置 URL 链接颜色
# linkcolor: red      # 设置内部跳转链接颜色（如目录）
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(kableExtra)

## Use the directory containing BKP_Slides.Rmd as the working directory
## for all R code chunks.
slides_dir <- dirname(
  normalizePath(
    knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE),
    winslash = "/"
  )
)

knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = slides_dir)
```

# Motivation


### Estimating an Unknown Probability Surface

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{ex2.pdf}
\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.5\textwidth}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Observation model}
\[
  y_i \mid \pi(\vx_i) \sim \operatorname{Binomial} \!\left(m_i,\pi(\vx_i)\right).
\]


\item \textbf{Objective}

Estimate the underlying probability surface \(\pi(\vx)\) and quantify its pointwise uncertainty.
\end{itemize}

\end{column}

\end{columns}


### Existing Approaches and the Remaining Gap

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.46\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Logistic regression}
\[
  \pi(\vx) = \operatorname{logit}^{-1} \!\left(\vx^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}\right)
\]

\begin{center}
\textcolor{red}{Fast and interpretable}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
\textcolor{blue}{Limited nonlinear flexibility}
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.46\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Gaussian process classification}
\[
  \pi(\vx)=\operatorname{logit}^{-1}\{f(\vx)\}, \quad f(\vx)\sim\operatorname{GP}
\]

\begin{center}
\textcolor{red}{Flexible probability surfaces}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
\textcolor{blue}{Approximate posterior inference}
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.8em}

\begin{alertblock}{Motivation}
Can we retain nonlinear flexibility while performing direct, closed-form updating on the probability scale?
\end{alertblock}

# Methodology

### Beta Kernel Process: Direct Probability-Scale Updating

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]
\hspace*{-0.02\textwidth}

% --------------------------------------------------
% Prior
% --------------------------------------------------
\begin{column}{0.28\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Beta prior}
\footnotesize
\[
  \pi(\vx) \sim \operatorname{Beta} \bigl\{\alpha_0(\vx),\beta_0(\vx) \bigr\}.
\]

\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{center}
Prior information on the probability scale
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.05\textwidth}
\centering
\Large
\[
+
\]
\end{column}

% --------------------------------------------------
% Local evidence
% --------------------------------------------------
\begin{column}{0.32\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Local likelihood}
\footnotesize
\[\begin{aligned}
  k_i(\vx) &= k(\vx,\vx_i;\vtheta),\\[0.4em]
  S(\vx) &= \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i(\vx)y_i,\\[0.4em]
  F(\vx) &= \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i(\vx)(m_i-y_i).
\end{aligned}\]

\vspace{0.2em}

\begin{center}
  Nearby observations receive greater weight
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\begin{column}{0.05\textwidth}
\centering
\Large
\[
\Rightarrow
\]
\end{column}

% --------------------------------------------------
% Posterior
% --------------------------------------------------
\begin{column}{0.33\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Beta posterior}

\footnotesize

\[\begin{aligned}
  \pi(\vx)\mid\calD &\sim \operatorname{Beta} \bigl\{\alpha_n(\vx),\beta_n(\vx)\bigr\},\\[0.4em]
  \alpha_n(\vx) &= \alpha_0(\vx)+S(\vx),\\[0.4em]
  \beta_n(\vx) &= \beta_0(\vx)+F(\vx).
\end{aligned}\]

\vspace{0.2em}

\begin{center}
Closed-form posterior inference
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{alertblock}{Key idea}
\centering
\textbf{Local kernel weighting}
\quad \(+\) \quad
\textbf{Beta--binomial conjugacy} \\
\(\Longrightarrow\) \quad
\textbf{flexible modeling with closed-form posterior inference}.
\end{alertblock}


### Closed-Form Posterior Summaries

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]
\begin{column}{0.35\textwidth}
\begin{block}{Probability estimation}
\footnotesize
\[
\widehat{\pi}(\vx)=\frac{\alpha_n(\vx)}{\alpha_n(\vx)+\beta_n(\vx)}
\]

\begin{center}
Posterior mean of the unknown probability surface
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.6\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Pointwise uncertainty}
\footnotesize
\[
\operatorname{Var}\{\pi(\vx)\mid\calD\}=
\frac{\widehat{\pi}(\vx)\{1-\widehat{\pi}(\vx)\}}
{\alpha_n(\vx)+\beta_n(\vx)+1}
\]
\[
\operatorname{CI}_{1-\delta}(\vx)=
\left[Q_{\delta/2}(\vx),Q_{1-\delta/2}(\vx)\right]
\]

\begin{center}
Credible intervals follow from Beta posterior quantiles
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.2em}
\begin{block}{Count prediction}
\small
\[
Y_{\mathrm{new}}\mid\calD,\vx,m_{\mathrm{new}}
\sim
\operatorname{Beta\text{-}Binomial}
\left\{m_{\mathrm{new}},\alpha_n(\vx),\beta_n(\vx)\right\}.
\]

\begin{center}
Closed-form prediction for future binomial counts
\end{center}

\end{block}


\vspace{0.2em}

\begin{alertblock}{Computational implication}
\centering
Conditional on the kernel hyperparameters, posterior summaries and predictive distributions are available without numerical posterior approximation.
\end{alertblock}

### Prior Specification

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.4\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Non-informative prior}
\small
\[
\pi(\vx)\sim\operatorname{Beta}(1,1).
\]

\begin{center}
Default uniform prior when no external information is available
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Informative prior with fixed mean}
\small
\[
\alpha_0(\vx)=r_0p_0, \quad \beta_0(\vx)=r_0(1-p_0)
\]
\[
\mathbb{E}\{\pi(\vx)\}=p_0, \quad \alpha_0(\vx)+\beta_0(\vx)=r_0
\]

\begin{center}
A fixed prior mean \(p_0\) with precision controlled by \(r_0\)
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.2em}
\begin{block}{Data-adaptive prior}
\small
\[
p(\vx)= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{k(\vx,\vx_i)}{\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}k(\vx,\vx_i)}\frac{y_i}{m_i}
, \quad r(\vx)=r_0\sum_{i=1}^{n}k(\vx,\vx_i)
\]
\[
\alpha_0(\vx)=r(\vx)p(\vx), \quad
\beta_0(\vx)=r(\vx)\{1-p(\vx)\}.
\]

\begin{center}
The prior mean follows the local empirical proportion, while the prior precision adapts to the local kernel mass
\end{center}

\end{block}


### Scaled Distance and Length-Scale Parameters

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Scaled distance}
\small
\[
h(\vx,\vx';\vtheta)=
\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}
\left(\frac{x_j-x_j'}{\theta_j}\right)^2}.
\]

\begin{itemize}
\item Smaller \(\theta_j\): more localized borrowing and greater sensitivity along dimension \(j\)
\item Larger \(\theta_j\): broader borrowing over longer distances
\end{itemize}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.47\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Length-scale structure}
\small
\[
\text{Isotropic:}\qquad
\theta_1=\cdots=\theta_d=\theta
\]
\[
\text{Anisotropic:}\qquad
\vtheta=(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_d)
\]

\begin{center}
An isotropic kernel uses a common length scale across dimensions, whereas an anisotropic kernel allows dimension-specific borrowing ranges
\end{center}

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{alertblock}{Modeling role}
\centering
The length-scale parameters determine how rapidly information borrowing decreases with distance.
\end{alertblock}


### Kernel Functions

\begin{block}{Implemented kernels}
\small
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gaussian:}\qquad
k(h) &= \exp(-h^2),\\[0.5em]
\text{Mat{\'e}rn }5/2:\qquad
k(h) &= \left(1+\sqrt{5}h+\frac{5}{3}h^2\right)\exp(-\sqrt{5}h),\\[0.5em]
\text{Mat{\'e}rn }3/2:\qquad
k(h) &= \left(1+\sqrt{3}h\right)\exp(-\sqrt{3}h),\\[0.5em]
\text{Wendland:}\qquad
k(h) &= (\zeta h+1)\max\{0,1-h\}^{\zeta}, \quad \zeta=\lfloor d/2\rfloor+3.
\end{aligned}
\]
\end{block}

\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.47\textwidth}
\begin{block}{Gaussian and Mat{\'e}rn}
\centering
Noncompact support

Every observation at a finite distance receives a positive weight, although the weight decreases with distance.
\end{block}
\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.47\textwidth}
\begin{block}{Wendland}
\centering
Compact support

Observations with \(h\geq 1\) receive exactly zero weight.
\end{block}
\end{column}

\end{columns}


### LOOCV-Based Length-Scale Selection

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.55\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Leave-one-out quantities}
\small
\begin{itemize}
\item \(\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)\): predicted probability at \(\vx_i\) after excluding observation \(i\).
\item \(\widetilde{\pi}_i=y_i/m_i\): observed empirical proportion.
\end{itemize}
\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.41\textwidth}

\begin{alertblock}{Selection criterion}
\small
\[
\widehat{\vtheta}
=
\underset{\vtheta}{\argmin}\,
L(\vtheta;\calD_n),
\quad
L\in\{\mathrm{BS},\mathrm{LL}\}.
\]
\end{alertblock}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{block}{Brier score}
\small
\[
\mathrm{BS}(\vtheta)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\left\{\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)-\widetilde{\pi}_i\right\}^{2}.
\]
\end{block}

\vspace{0.15em}

\begin{block}{Log-loss}
\small
\[
\mathrm{LL}(\vtheta)=-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\left[
\widetilde{\pi}_i\log\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)
+(1-\widetilde{\pi}_i)\log\{1-\widehat{\pi}^{-i}(\vx_i;\vtheta)\}
\right].
\]
\end{block}

\begin{center}
\footnotesize
All leave-one-out predictions are obtained from one kernel matrix by setting its diagonal entries to zero.
\end{center}


### Multi-Start Hyperparameter Optimization

\begin{columns}[c,onlytextwidth]

\begin{column}{0.42\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Log-scale reparameterization}
\small
\[
\gamma_j=\log_{10}(\theta_j),
\quad
\theta_j=10^{\gamma_j}.
\]
\[
d_\gamma=
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{isotropic},\\
d, & \text{anisotropic}.
\end{cases}
\]

\begin{center}
The transformation enforces positive length-scale parameters
\end{center}
\end{block}

\end{column}

\hfill

\begin{column}{0.53\textwidth}

\begin{block}{Optimization workflow}

\begin{enumerate}

  \item Generate \(n_0=10d_\gamma\) initial values using a Latin hypercube design

  \item Run the derivative-free SBPLX algorithm from each initial value

  \item Retain the solution with the smallest LOOCV loss

  \end{enumerate}
\[
  s^\star= \underset{1\leq s\leq n_0}{\argmin}\; L\!\left(\widehat{\vgamma}^{(s)};\calD\right),
\qquad \widehat{\vgamma} = \widehat{\vgamma}^{(s^\star)}.
\]

\end{block}

\end{column}

\end{columns}

\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{alertblock}{Why multi-start?}
\centering
The LOOCV objective may be non-convex, so multiple initial values reduce sensitivity to a single local optimum.
\end{alertblock}





























---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='60%'}
knitr::include_graphics("Thanks.png")
```
